screens.title: Index laws become useful when expressions look messy
screens.body: In TMUA-style questions, index laws are usually hidden inside algebraic manipulation. You may need to rewrite fractions, roots, and powers before you can compare values, solve equations, or differentiate.
screens.keyPoint: A useful rewrite is: xa ÷ xb = xa-b. Also, 1xn = x-n and √x = x1/2 for x ≥ 0.

screens.title: Make the expression easier before doing anything else

steps.title: Start with the denominator
steps.body: If you see x√x, rewrite it as x × x<sup>1/2</sup> = x<sup>3/2</sup>.

screens.question: For all positive real numbers a and b, √(a<sup>2</sup> + b<sup>2</sup>) = a + b.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Surds do not distribute over addition. For example, if a = 3 and b = 4, then √(3² + 4²) = 5, but 3 + 4 = 7.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Surds do not distribute over addition. A counterexample is a = 3 and b = 4: √(3² + 4²) = 5, but a + b = 7.

screens.question: For x > 0, which expression is equal to x2 - 4x√x?
screens.prompt: Rewrite the denominator using powers of x.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. x√x = x<sup>3/2</sup>, so the expression becomes x<sup>2-3/2</sup> - 4x<sup>-3/2</sup> = x<sup>1/2</sup> - 4x<sup>-3/2</sup>.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. First rewrite x√x as x<sup>3/2</sup>. Then split the fraction: x<sup>2</sup> ÷ x<sup>3/2</sup> = x<sup>1/2</sup>, and 4 ÷ x<sup>3/2</sup> = 4x<sup>-3/2</sup>.

screens.title: Index laws before differentiation
screens.question: Given f(x) = x2 - 4x√x for x > 0, which option is f′(x)?

steps.title: Match the option
steps.body: So f′(x) = 12x-1/2 + 6x-5/2, which is option A.

screens.finalAnswer: The answer is option A. The main trick is simplifying the expression before differentiating.

screens.title: Indices handled.
screens.body: You have used index laws in a more TMUA-style setting: not just simplifying, but preparing an expression for another step.

screens.question: Which of the following has the smallest numerical value?
screens.prompt: This is a no-calculator comparison. Convert each option into a simple value.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The values are 3/8, 1/13, 1/2, and √0.008. Since 1/13 is about 0.077, option B is the smallest.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. A = 1/4 + 1/8 = 3/8. B = 1/(4+9) = 1/13. C = 1/2. D = √0.008, which is larger than 1/13. The smallest is B.

screens.question: If x<sup>a</sup> = x<sup>b</sup>, then a = b for all real a and b whenever x > 0.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. If x = 1, then 1<sup>a</sup> = 1<sup>b</sup> for any a and b. For example, 1² = 1³, but 2 ≠ 3.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. The statement fails when x = 1. Then 1<sup>a</sup> = 1<sup>b</sup> is always true, even if a and b are different.

screens.title: Exponential equations by substitution
screens.question: The sum of the real roots of 4<sup>x</sup> - 6(2<sup>x</sup>) + 8 = 0 is:

steps.title: Use substitution
steps.body: Let y = 2<sup>x</sup>. The equation becomes y<sup>2</sup> - 6y + 8 = 0.

steps.title: Answer the actual question
steps.body: The question asks for the sum of the real roots, so 2 + 1 = 3.

screens.finalAnswer: The answer is 3, so the correct option is B.

screens.title: You spotted the hidden quadratic.
screens.body: That substitution pattern appears often: when a power like 4ˣ can be rewritten using 2ˣ, the equation may secretly be quadratic.

screens.title: Factor theorem with an unknown constant
screens.question: Given that (x + 2) is a factor of f(x) = x<sup>3</sup> + kx<sup>2</sup> - 4x - 8, which option gives k and the roots of f(x) = 0?
options: k = 2; roots are x = 2 and x = -2 repeated
k = -2; roots are x = 2 and x = -2 repeated

steps.title: Use the factor theorem
steps.body: If (x + 2) is a factor, then f(-2) = 0.

screens.finalAnswer: The correct option is A. k = 2, and the roots are x = 2 and x = -2 repeated.

screens.title: Indices, Surds and Polynomials: key points
keyPoints: Rewrite surds and denominators using rational or negative powers before manipulating expressions.
For x > 0, √x = x<sup>1/2</sup>, and x√x = x<sup>3/2</sup>.
Hidden quadratic equations can appear when expressions like 4<sup>x</sup> are rewritten as (2<sup>x</sup>)<sup>2</sup>.
Use the Factor Theorem by substituting the root into the polynomial.
Be careful with traps: powers and roots do not distribute over addition.
reviewItems: Practise simplifying algebraic fractions before differentiating.
Review exponential equations solved using substitution.
Practise factor theorem questions involving unknown constants.
